questions.question: Calculate the time taken for a microwave signal to reach a geostationary satellite at an altitude of $36000\text{ km}$. ($c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$)
questions.explanation:
Using $t = s/v$, $t = 36000000\text{ m} / 300000000\text{ m/s} = 0.12\text{ s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert km to m, resulting in a much smaller value.

questions.question: Why is it an advantage that mobile phones use microwaves rather than long-wavelength radio waves?
options: Microwaves require only a short aerial
Microwaves are less harmful to the brain
Microwaves can travel through hills
Microwaves do not require a line-of-sight
questions.explanation:
The source states that for microwaves used in mobile phones, only a short aerial is needed. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking microwaves are completely harmless; excessive exposure can cause internal heating.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes a benefit of digital signaling over long distances?
options: The signal can be regenerated without including noise
The signal travels much faster than an analogue signal
The signal is immune to all forms of interference
Digital signals do not require a medium to travel
questions.explanation:
A major benefit of digital signaling is that the signal can be regenerated cleanly at stations, whereas analogue signals amplify noise. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking digital signals travel faster; both travel at the speed of light.

questions.question: Compared to copper wires, optical fibres are better for telecommunication because:
options: They carry more information and have less signal loss
They are cheaper to manufacture and install
They use sound waves which are safer for the environment
They work even when there is a complete power failure
questions.explanation:
Optical fibres can carry more data and experience lower signal loss than traditional copper wires. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting Choice B; while glass is common, the precision of optical fibre systems is often more expensive.

questions.question: What happens to a signal when it is 'regenerated' in a digital system?
options: The original pulses are identified and a clean new signal is produced
The signal is amplified along with any noise it picked up
The signal is converted into an analogue wave for the next stage
The frequency of the signal is doubled to increase speed
questions.explanation:
Regeneration involves creating a fresh version of the digital pulses (0s and 1s), removing any distortion or noise. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking it is just simple amplification.
